summary: Stancil argues that analysts can resemble “nicer, kinder” Jared Kushners: parachuting into others’ problems with shallow context and overconfident analysis that adds little value. He suggests shifting data teams from decision-support consultants to newsroom-style publishers who build company-wide, ambient understanding through continual narrative updates. A better-informed organization, not better point decisions, is the real competitive edge.
money_quotes: A crash course, no matter how intense and thorough, would be a poor substitute for just reading the news for twenty years.
Our involvement just isn’t that useful, and we’re mostly saying things they already know.
Dashboards lack narratives, and those narratives are often more important than the metrics themselves.
Instead of being researchers and scientists who make the news, data teams could be journalists and editors who report on it.
Without curation, publication, and distribution, this could, counterintuitively, make a company less informed.
tone: reflective, critical, and analytical with humor
key_insight: Data teams should prioritize publishing continuous, narrative context that keeps everyone informed, rather than parachuting into discrete decisions with shallow analysis.
